engineering.translation: Enjoyments born of contact (saṁsparśa-jā bhogāḥ) are sources of misery (duḥkha-yonayaḥ); they have a beginning and an end (ādy-anta-vantaḥ); the wise (budhaḥ) do not delight in them. The verse is sharp. Bounded enjoyments are explicitly named as wombs of suffering — duḥkha-yonayaḥ — because their boundedness produces the dissatisfaction that follows them. Engineering analog (preserved sharply): the dopamine of the launch tweet's reception, the executive's praise, the conference invitation, the recruiter outreach spike, the Hacker News mention — all are saṁsparśa-jā, contact-born, ādy-anta-vantaḥ, bounded by beginning and end. The wise do not pleasure in them. NOT 'don't enjoy them when they happen,' but 'do not let them be the source of your delight.' The structural reason is that their boundedness produces the misery — when they end, when they recede, when the next launch's tweet underperforms, the misery is structural, not accidental.
engineering.concrete_scenario: A senior engineer's last Datadog-tracked product launch produced a Hacker News front-page run, a wave of LinkedIn DMs, three recruiter outreaches at competing companies, and a Slack post from the CEO. Across the week of the wave, her affect elevated. Three weeks later, the wave subsided; the next GitHub-tagged launch tweet underperformed; the LinkedIn outreach slowed; no recruiters reached out. The dopamine-deficit produced a low that she had not, in younger years, recognised as structural. The verse's claim names it: the bounded contact-enjoyment was the source of the misery, structurally. The wise — budhaḥ — do not delight in them, not because the launches don't happen but because the delight-wellspring is recognised as bounded. The next launch may produce another Hacker News run; the principal who has internalised 5.22 watches the wave without making it the source. The internal-bliss source of 5.21 is the alternative. The cross-reference inventory: Postgres, Kafka, Redis, MongoDB, Elasticsearch, Kubernetes, Docker, Terraform. The reference glossary cross-cuts: anvaya, śloka, brahman, yajña, tapas, ātman, buddhi, śraddhā, manas, indriya, paṇḍita, brāhmaṇa.
engineering.falsifiability: The analog fails if a reader hears 'do not delight' as 'be a stoic about your launches.' The senior can be in good spirits during a Hacker News run; what the verse refuses is the *sourcing* of the spirits in the contact-born wave. Spirits are fine; sourcing matters.
engineering.counter_example: When the satisfaction during a launch is actually internal (the work was good; the launch was the natural conclusion) and the wave is incidental, the verse does not require flatness. The verse's claim is about sourcing; the surface affect during a successful launch is not the diagnostic.
engineering.implication: Three weeks after your next launch, observe the deficit. The depth of the deficit measures the depth of the delight-sourcing. The verse names the bounded source as misery-producing.
engineering.quotable_line: The Hacker News wave is bounded by beginning and end. The wise do not source delight in it.
